text: The surgeon demonstrates the port placement for a hand-assisted nephrectomy on the right side. The patient is positioned in a beanbag in a full-flank position. A small seven-centimeter muscle-splitting incision is made for the hand port, with two additional ten-millimeter ports placed, including one inside the umbilicus. A three-millimeter alligator grasper is used to elevate the liver.

The surgeon places their hand through the hand port, ensuring a tight seal for laparoscopic insufflation. A three-millimeter grasper is positioned as a self-retaining retractor for the liver, without requiring an additional port. The gallbladder, liver, and kidney are clearly visible in the thin patient.
The surgeon begins the nephrectomy by performing a formal takedown of the line of Toldt, which involves mobilizing the colon to access the kidney.

The surgeon rapidly dissects the line of Toldt using a harmonic scalpel, extending the dissection towards the side of the liver and diaphragm. The hand is positioned over the top of the kidney, sweeping the Gerota's fascia downwards, revealing the kidney in full view.

The surgeon uses their hand to sweep the peritoneum downwards along the liver edge, extending towards the incision made near the diaphragm, demonstrating the advantage of hand-assisted technique in nephrectomy.
The surgeon uses their hand as a retractor to expose the top of the kidney. They proceed to take down the duodenum and colon, identifying the plane where the colon rests on the Gerota's fascia.
The surgeon mobilizes the colon downwards on the right side, revealing the underlying bowel, likely the duodenum. Fingers are used as a dissector to position the colon for retraction.

The surgeon uses two fingers as a retractor while employing the pinky finger to push the kidney laterally and the thumb to push the bowel medially, creating retraction points for better exposure during the nephrectomy.

The surgeon carefully navigates into the anatomical plane, ensuring the duodenum is moved aside. Using a hand-assisted technique, the surgeon systematically identifies and exposes necessary structures by finding and expanding tissue planes.

The renal vein becomes visible as the surgeon frees up the lower pole of the kidney. With the duodenum and bowel retracted, the surgeon completes the mobilization of the colon, extending the dissection down to the pelvis.
The surgeon uses their hand to hold the kidney upright, revealing the vena cava and an aberrant crossing vessel. The lower pole of the kidney is elevated while the colon is being mobilized.
The surgeon continues to march up the vena cava, revealing a small crossing vessel, possibly an aberrant gonadal vessel or one leading to the kidney, without focusing on dissecting it.
The surgeon positions onto the psoas muscle and begins to move upwards, approaching the renal vein previously identified. Several aberrant vessels are visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon holds the kidney upright using the thumb and forefinger, revealing a small crossing artery and vein. With the duodenum positioned down, a distinct plane of fascia is visible, beneath which the main renal vein can be clearly observed.

The surgeon follows a predictable fascial plane beneath the plane used to mobilize the duodenum, moving across the top of the kidney. Gyrotis fat is released in this area to spare the adrenal gland. The renal vein is visible, beginning to branch.

The surgeon gently exposes the parenchyma of the kidney, ensuring no vascular structures remain. The Girotis fat has been swept down from the upper pole, and windows are created in this fat. The surgeon prepares to staple the fat, noting that the adrenal gland is located closer to the vena cava. The fat is prone to bleeding, particularly on the left side, so a stapler is used for efficient and safe management.

The surgeon completes dissection around the upper pole of the kidney, ensuring the adrenal gland is spared. The kidney is held upright, revealing the renal vein. The surgeon places fingers behind the hilum to dissect and isolate the visible vessels encountered during the procedure.
The surgeon works on a crossing vessel located inferiorly, using their thumb as a retractor to hold the kidney straight up, creating visible retraction points.

The surgeon uses their middle finger to encircle the hilum, pushing the artery out from underneath the vein to facilitate vessel dissection. The artery is clearly visible as it is pushed aside. The surgeon then proceeds retroperitoneally along the psoas muscle to fully mobilize the kidney, preparing to hold it upright.

The surgeon identifies the psoas muscle and notes that the hilum has not been fully dissected yet. The hilum is identified to manage potential bleeding, with the surgeon preferring to address it as the final step to ensure all vessels are visible and avoid complications. The current focus is on releasing the lateral attachments of the kidney.

The surgeon identifies the ureter for the first time during the procedure, revealing an aberrant gonadal vessel alongside a crossing artery and vein. The surgeon carefully navigates these structures while continuing the dissection.

The surgeon's finger is positioned underneath the artery, pulling it out from behind the renal vein. The artery becomes more visible as the finger creates space for the stapler, preparing to secure the vessel.

The surgeon creates a clear window by positioning their finger on both sides of the vessels, ensuring upward traction on the kidney. This maneuver allows safe passage of the stapler without obstruction from surrounding structures. The vessels are under tension, preventing any manipulation towards the mesentery of the bowel.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to address lymphatic tissue while preparing the kidney for staple firing. Several aberrant arteries and veins are visible, which are identified and managed as part of the hand-assisted procedure.
The ureter is visible, and the surgeon begins to take tissue on the side of the ureter. Small aberrant vessels are secured using small metal clips.
The surgeon prepares to take the main renal artery, using an automatic firing stapler to secure the vessel before proceeding to the veins.

The surgeon identifies and prepares to take an aberrant crossing artery, which is atypical and not obstructing the renal pelvis. The artery is isolated, and the remaining veins are visible, with care taken not to approach too closely to the vena cava before branching.
The surgeon isolates the remaining lymphatics and a single vein, preparing to address these vessels with precision.

The surgeon prepares to take the remaining vein, leaving the ureter as the final structure to be addressed. Metal sutures are typically used for the ureter, with wet clips considered if the ureter is hydronephrotic or if reflux is suspected.

The surgeon presents the kidney specimen, which is extracted from the abdomen without visible tumor due to its centralized location, allowing for straightforward removal without the need for a lap sac.

The surgeon places a sponge to address minimal bleeding in the surgical field, inspecting the stumps for any signs of oozing or bleeding. The insufflation pressure is reduced to five to ensure hemostasis.
The video shows the staple line on the renal vein adjacent to the vena cava. The accessory artery and the stump of the artery are visible, with no signs of leakage, indicating satisfactory closure.
The surgeon inspects the bivalve kidney specimen, revealing the centrally located tumor. The sponge used in the procedure remains white, indicating no active bleeding.
